messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing a psychological case vignette
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

You have been given a patient's full PTSD profile including their demographics, specific self-reported symptoms, and causal connections between components.
Use ALL of this information to construct a realistic and personalised clinical portrait.

Patient demographics:
  age: 58
  gender: Male
  ethnicity: South Asian
  relationship_status: Married
  occupation: Plumber
  trauma_type: Terrorism
  pcl5: 80

Patient-reported symptoms per component:
  Triggers: Nighttime/Dark: Night-time or waking up in the dark, Auditory (Explosion): Explosion or bang sounds, Loss of Control: Lack of control
  Threat: Scanning Exits: I scan for exits as soon as I enter any room or building, Threat in Faces: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat, Alert at Home: I feel on guard even inside my own home
  Memory: Snapshot Memory: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind., Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily., Story Feels Unreal: The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me
  Negative Appraisals: Mistrust: People can't be trusted, Isolation: I feel isolated and set apart from others, Outsider: I am permanently an outsider now

Active causal connections (must appear in the vignette):
  - Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Triggers → Threat
  - Triggers → Memory
  - Triggers → Negative Appraisals
  - Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Negative Appraisals → Threat
  - Negative Appraisals → Memory
  - Negative Appraisals → Triggers
  - Memory → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Memory → Threat
  - Memory → Negative Appraisals
  - Memory → Triggers
  - Threat → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Threat → Memory
  - Threat → Negative Appraisals
  - Threat → Triggers
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Memory
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals

Forbidden causal connections (must NEVER appear — not explicitly, not implicitly, not narratively):
  (none)

Constraints:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not in the active list.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". Both components may appear in the vignette — just do not connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y", "she steers clear of X, fearing that Y"). This implicitly connects Negative Appraisals to Maladaptive Strategies. Instead, describe the avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground the vignette in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate symptoms.

Output Format:
Write 200–300 words in third person.
Cover: presenting complaints, trauma background, cognitive distortions, avoidance, and maintaining factors.
Avoid excessive jargon - write for a clinical case conference audience.

Write a clinical vignette for this patient.

The vignette should read as a cohesive, flowing portrait — not a structured report.
Weave together who this person is, what happened to them, how they experience their
symptoms day to day, how they cope, and what toll this takes on their life and relationships.

Let causes and consequences emerge naturally through the narrative rather than being
stated explicitly. Reflect the strength of causal connections through emphasis and
narrative weight — stronger links should feel more central to the story.

Do not use section headers, numbered points, or clinical labels for symptoms.
Write in the third person, in a tone suitable for presenting a case to a clinical supervisor.
Aim for 4–5 paragraphs of continuous prose.

He is a 58-year-old South Asian married man who works as a plumber and presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following a terrorist incident. His distress is captured by a very high PCL-5 score of 80. In day-to-day life he is drawn back to the event by specific cues: night-time or waking in the dark leaves him tense, sudden explosion or bang sounds make him jolt, and any sense of lacking control quickly unsettles him. These moments are followed by a marked sense of threat. He scans exits as soon as he enters a room or building, studies people’s faces and body language for danger, and remains on guard even inside his own home.

He describes the traumatic memory in an unusual way: it is held as a series of frightening snapshots, each moment vivid like a picture, yet the whole event can be spoken about easily. At the same time, the experience feels so extreme that it is hard for him to believe it actually happened to him. He notices that when reminders arise, the memory becomes more intrusive and his sense of danger intensifies, while his threat monitoring seems to pull him back toward fragments of the event and into the same alarmed state.

His outlook on others has become increasingly negative. He says people cannot be trusted, feels isolated and set apart from others, and experiences himself as permanently an outsider now. These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to his ongoing alertness, his repeated return to the traumatic memory, and the way reminders appear in everyday life. Triggers at night and in the dark, and explosive sounds in particular, have become especially potent in keeping the pattern active.

He copes by keeping watch, checking environments, and staying emotionally and physically guarded. These strategies bring little relief and appear to leave him more fixed on danger and more trapped with the trauma memory. He has become increasingly withdrawn, and this sense of disconnection has strained his relationships at home and narrowed his daily functioning. Overall, his presentation reflects a self-reinforcing cycle in which reminders, threat monitoring, the trauma memory, and mistrustful appraisals all remain tightly intertwined.

output:
He is a 58-year-old South Asian married man who works as a plumber and presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following a terrorist incident. His distress is captured by a very high PCL-5 score of 80. In day-to-day life he is drawn back to the event by specific cues: night-time or waking in the dark leaves him tense, sudden explosion or bang sounds make him jolt, and any sense of lacking control quickly unsettles him. These moments are followed by a marked sense of threat. He scans exits as soon as he enters a room or building, studies people’s faces and body language for danger, and remains on guard even inside his own home.

He describes the traumatic memory in an unusual way: it is held as a series of frightening snapshots, each moment vivid like a picture, yet the whole event can be spoken about easily. At the same time, the experience feels so extreme that it is hard for him to believe it actually happened to him. He notices that when reminders arise, the memory becomes more intrusive and his sense of danger intensifies, while his threat monitoring seems to pull him back toward fragments of the event and into the same alarmed state.

His outlook on others has become increasingly negative. He says people cannot be trusted, feels isolated and set apart from others, and experiences himself as permanently an outsider now. These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to his ongoing alertness, his repeated return to the traumatic memory, and the way reminders appear in everyday life. Triggers at night and in the dark, and explosive sounds in particular, have become especially potent in keeping the pattern active.

He copes by keeping watch, checking environments, and staying emotionally and physically guarded. These strategies bring little relief and appear to leave him more fixed on danger and more trapped with the trauma memory. He has become increasingly withdrawn, and this sense of disconnection has strained his relationships at home and narrowed his daily functioning. Overall, his presentation reflects a self-reinforcing cycle in which reminders, threat monitoring, the trauma memory, and mistrustful appraisals all remain tightly intertwined.
